title: Reading and Use of English – Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Psychology of Peak Performance
content.subtitle: Beyond talent: Exploring the mental frameworks of elite athletes and high achievers.
content.text: For decades, the search for the secret to sporting excellence focused almost exclusively on the physiological: lung capacity, muscle fiber composition, and metabolic efficiency. However, a paradigm shift in sport psychology has revealed that at the highest levels of competition, the physical differences between athletes are often negligible. [31] Success is increasingly seen as a 'mental game,' where the ability to regulate one's internal state under extreme pressure is the true arbiter of victory. [/31] This transition from the physical to the psychological has opened up new avenues for training, with top-tier performers now spending as much time in 'mental gyms' as they do on the track or in the pool. || A cornerstone of this mental training is the concept of 'arousal regulation.' Every athlete has an 'Optimal Zone of Functioning'—a specific level of physiological and emotional activation where they perform at their best. [32] For some, this requires a state of calm, almost meditative focus, while others need a surge of adrenaline and high-intensity aggression to break through their limits. [/32] The challenge lies in the fact that the pressure of a major final naturally pushes the brain toward 'hyper-arousal,' leading to the dreaded 'choke,' where fine motor skills are compromised by over-active conscious thought. Learning to stay in the zone is, therefore, a matter of mastery over the nervous system. || [33] Another critical element is 'perceptual-cognitive skill,' which refers to an athlete's ability to locate and identify environmental cues to anticipate future events. [/33] In sports like tennis or cricket, where the ball travels at speeds exceeding human reaction time, elite players do not rely on faster reflexes. Instead, they have developed the ability to 'read' the opponent's body language—the angle of a shoulder or the flick of a wrist—to predict where the ball will land before it has even been struck. This 'expert advantage' is essentially a highly developed form of pattern recognition, built through thousands of hours of deliberate practice. || However, the mental burden of constant improvement can lead to burnout, a state of emotional and physical exhaustion. [34] Critics of the 'win-at-all-costs' mentality suggest that the relentless pursuit of peak performance can strip the joy from the sport, turning a passion into a chore. [/34] To counter this, many psychologists now emphasize the importance of 'psychological flexibility'—the ability to stay in the present moment even when experiencing difficult thoughts or setbacks. Rather than trying to suppress anxiety, athletes are taught to acknowledge it and pivot their focus back to the task at hand, a technique borrowed heavily from mindfulness traditions. || The role of 'self-talk' has also come under intense scrutiny. Traditional coaching often relied on 'positive affirmations'—telling oneself 'I can do this.' [35] Yet, recent studies suggest that 'interrogative self-talk'—asking oneself 'Can I do this?'—is actually more effective, as it prompts the brain to search for strategies and solutions rather than just providing a temporary emotional boost. [/35] It shifts the athlete from a passive state of hope to an active state of problem-solving. This subtle shift in internal dialogue can be the difference between crumbling under pressure and finding a way to adapt to a losing situation. || Looking ahead, the integration of technology, such as neurofeedback and virtual reality, promises to take mental training to even greater heights. [36] The author's tone indicates a belief that these tools will eventually democratize peak performance, making elite mental strategies accessible to amateur enthusiasts. [/36] While the ethics of 'cognitive enhancement' in sports remain a topic of heated debate, the fundamental truth remains: the most powerful muscle in any athlete's body is the one between their ears. As we continue to unlock the mysteries of the sporting mind, the boundaries of what humans can achieve will likely continue to expand, proving that the limit of our performance is rarely dictated by our bodies alone, but by the constraints we place on our own potential.

questions.question: In the first paragraph, the writer suggests that at the elite level...
options: A) physical training has become completely obsolete.
B) differences in lung capacity are the primary cause of failure.
C) psychological factors are the most decisive element in winning.
D) athletes are spending less time on physical exercise than in the past.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [31], stating mental regulation is the 'true arbiter of victory.' A and D are incorrect because the text says mental training is 'as much' as physical, not replacing it. B is contradicted by the text.

questions.question: What point is made about the 'Optimal Zone of Functioning' in the second paragraph?
options: A) It is a universal state of calm that all athletes must achieve.
B) It varies between individuals, requiring different emotional states.
C) It is impossible to maintain during a major sporting final.
D) It is primarily triggered by a sudden surge of adrenaline.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [32], which explains that some need calm while others need aggression. A and D are incorrect as they only describe one side of the spectrum. C is wrong because the text says the 'challenge' is staying in it, not that it's impossible.

questions.question: According to the third paragraph, 'perceptual-cognitive skill' allows athletes to...
options: A) react faster to physical stimuli than the average person.
B) ignore environmental cues to focus purely on their own movements.
C) use pattern recognition to foresee an opponent's actions.
D) improve their vision through deliberate practice.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [33] and the explanation of 'reading' body language to 'predict' events. A is explicitly denied ('do not rely on faster reflexes'). B is the opposite of what is stated. D refers to cognitive 'reading,' not physical eyesight improvement.

questions.question: What does the author suggest about the 'win-at-all-costs' mentality?
options: A) It is the most effective way to ensure long-term athletic success.
B) It can have a negative impact on an athlete's emotional well-being.
C) It has been successfully replaced by mindfulness in all major sports.
D) It is only problematic for athletes who lack psychological flexibility.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [34], mentioning it can 'strip the joy' and turn passion into a 'chore.' A is contradicted. C and D are not supported by the text's more nuanced discussion.

questions.question: The writer mentions 'interrogative self-talk' to show that...
options: A) positive affirmations are a waste of time for professional athletes.
B) athletes should be more critical of their own performances.
C) questioning one's ability can lead to more effective problem-solving.
D) internal dialogue has a limited impact on actual sporting outcomes.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [35], stating it 'prompts the brain to search for strategies.' A is too extreme. B misinterprets 'interrogative' as 'critical.' D is the opposite of the author's point.

questions.question: In the final paragraph, the author's tone toward new technology in sports is...
options: A) fearful of the ethical implications of cognitive enhancement.
B) skeptical about the effectiveness of virtual reality training.
C) optimistic about its potential to help a wider range of people.
D) indifferent, as the 'mental game' cannot be improved by machines.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [36], which mentions the belief that tools will 'democratize peak performance.' A is incorrect because while he acknowledges the 'heated debate,' his own tone is described as 'optimism.' B and D are not supported.
